en.extract:
The African penguin (Spheniscus demersus), also known as Cape penguin or South African penguin, is a species of penguin confined to southern African waters. It is the only penguin found in the Old World. Like all penguins, it is flightless, with a streamlined body and wings stiffened and flattened into flippers for a marine habitat. Adults weigh an average of 2.2–3.5 kg (4.9–7.7 lb) and are 60–70 cm (24–28 in) tall. The species has distinctive pink patches of skin above the eyes and a black facial mask. The body's upper parts are black and sharply delineated from the white underparts, which are spotted and marked with a black band.
The African penguin is a pursuit diver and feeds primarily on fish and squid. Once extremely numerous, the African penguin is now the rarest species of penguin, classified as critically endangered, with its population declining rapidly due to a combination of several threats, such as habitat loss, overfishing and climate change. It is a charismatic species and is popular with tourists. Other vernacular names of the species include black-footed penguin and jackass penguin, due to the species' loud, donkey-like noise (although several related species of South American penguins produce the same sound). They can be found along the coast of South Africa and Namibia.


Taxonomy
The English naturalist George Edwards included an illustration and a description of the African penguin in the second volume of his A Natural History of Uncommon Birds in 1747. He used the English name "The Black-Footed Penguins". Edwards based his hand-coloured etching on two preserved specimens that had been brought to London. He suspected that they had been collected near the Cape of Good Hope. In 1758, when the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the tenth edition, he placed the African penguin with the wandering albatross in the genus Diomedea. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Diomedea demersa and cited Edwards' work. The African penguin is now placed with the banded penguins in the genus Spheniscus that was introduced in 1760 by the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson. The genus name Spheniscus is from Ancient Greek word σφήν (sphēn) meaning "wedge" and is a reference to the animal's thin, wedge-shaped flippers. The specific epithet demersus is Latin meaning "plunging" (from demergere meaning "to sink").
Banded penguins are found mainly in the temperate Southern Hemisphere, with the Humboldt penguin and Magellanic penguin found in southern South America and the Galápagos penguin found in the Pacific Ocean near the equator. All are similar in shape, colour and behaviour.


Description

African penguins grow to 60–70 cm (24–28 in) tall and weigh between 2.2–3.5 kg (4.9–7.7 lb). The beak length of the African penguin varies, usually growing between 4.8–6.5 cm (1.9–2.6 in). They have a black stripe and black spots on the chest, the pattern of which is unique to each penguin, like human fingerprints. The sweat glands above the eyes cool the birds' blood and as the temperature rises, increased blood flow causes the glands to get pinker. This species exhibits slight sexual dimorphism; the males are slightly larger than the females and have longer beaks. Juveniles do not possess the bold, delineated markings of the adult, but instead have dark upperparts that vary from greyish-blue to brown; the pale underparts lack both spots and the band. The beak is more pointed than that of the Humboldt penguin. The African penguin's colouring is a form of protective colouration known as countershading. The white undersides of the birds are difficult to spot by predators under the water and the penguins' black backs blend in with the water when viewed from above. African penguins possess supraorbital salt glands located above the eyes that allow them to excrete excess salt from ingesting seawater, an adaptation that helps maintain osmotic balance while foraging at sea.
African penguins resemble and are related to the Humboldt, Magellanic and Galápagos penguins. African penguins have a very recognisable appearance, with a thick band of black that is in the shape of an upside-down horseshoe. They have black feet and black spots that vary in size and shape between individuals. Magellanic penguins share a similar bar marking that often confuses the two; the Magellanic has a double bar on the throat and chest, whereas the African has a single bar. These penguins are sometimes called "jackass penguins", which comes from the loud braying noises they make.


Distribution
The African penguin is found on the southern and southwestern coast of Africa, living in colonies on 27 islands between Namibia and Algoa Bay, near Port Elizabeth, South Africa. It is the only penguin species that breeds in Africa, and its presence gave name to the Penguin Islands.

Two colonies were established by penguins in the 1980s on the mainland near Cape Town, namely Boulders Beach near Simon's Town and Stony Point in Betty's Bay. Mainland colonies likely became possible only in recent times due to the reduction of predator numbers, although the Betty's Bay colony has been attacked by leopards. The only other mainland colony is in Namibia, but it is not known when it was established.
Boulders Beach is a tourist attraction due to the beach, swimming, and the penguins themselves. The penguins will allow people to approach them as close as a meter.
Breeding populations of African penguins are being kept in numerous zoos worldwide. No colonies are known outside the southwestern coast of Africa, although vagrants (mostly juveniles) may occasionally be sighted beyond the normal range.


Population
Roughly 4 million African penguins existed at the beginning of the 19th century. Of the 1.5 million African penguins estimated in 1910, only some 10% remained at the end of the 20th century. African penguin populations, which breed in Namibia and South Africa, have declined by 95% since pre-industrial times.
Today, breeding is largely restricted to 24 islands from Namibia to Algoa Bay, South Africa, with the Boulders Beach colony being an exception to this rule. The total population fell to approximately 150,000–180,000 in 2000. Of those, 56,000 belonged to the Dassen Island colony and 14,000 to the Robben Island colony. The colony at Dyer Island in South Africa fell from 46,000 in the early 1970s to 3,000 in 2008.
In 2008, 5,000 breeding pairs were estimated to live in Namibia.
In 2010, the total African penguin population was estimated at 55,000. At the rate of decline seen from 2000 to 2010, the African penguin was expected to be extinct in the wild by 2026.
In 2012, about 18,700 breeding pairs were estimated to live in South Africa, with the majority on St. Croix Island in Algoa Bay.
The total breeding population across both South Africa and Namibia fell to about 20,850 pairs in 2019 and further declined to below 10,000 pairs in 2023. At this point the species was reclassified as critically endangered, with the suggestion that it would become functionally extinct in about 2035 if the current trajectory continued. Despite expert recommendations for immediate and wide-ranging closures of breeding areas to fishing, the South African government chose to merely retain a number of previously instituted trial closures that had been shown to be largely ineffective. In response, SANCOBB and BirdLife South Africa together with the Biodiversity Law Centre launched a landmark litigation to revise the decision, based on invoking the government's constitutional obligation to prevent extinction of an endangered species. On 18 March 2025, prior to the court hearing, a settlement was reached in which the Ministry set aside its earlier decision and decreed the establishment of a set of larger and full-time no-fishing zones around six key breeding areas.


Behaviour


Diet
African penguins forage in the open sea, where they feed on pelagic fish such as sardines (including the blue pilchard), South African mullet, Cape horse mackerels, red-eye round herrings and anchovies (specifically the European anchovy and the Southern African anchovy) and marine invertebrates such as squids (Loligo reynaudi) and small crustaceans, primarily krills, shrimps and Pterygosquilla armata. Penguins normally swim within 20 km (12 mi) of the shore. A penguin may consume up to 540 grams (1.19 lb) of prey every day, but this may increase to over 1 kg (2.2 lb) when raising older chicks.
Due to the marked decline of sardines in the waters near its habitat, African penguins' diet has shifted towards anchovies to some extent, although available sardine biomass is still a notable determinant of penguin population development and breeding success. While a diet of anchovies appears to be generally sufficient for the penguins, it is not ideal due to anchovies' lower concentrations of fat and protein. The species' diet changes throughout the year; as in many seabirds, it is believed that the interaction of diet choice and breeding success helps the penguins maintain their population size. Although parent penguins are protective of their chicks, they will not incur nutritional deficits themselves if food is scarce and hunting requires a greater time or energy commitment. This may lead to higher rates of brood loss under poor food conditions.
When foraging, African penguins carry out dives that reach an average depth of 25 m (82 ft) and last for 69 seconds, although a maximum depth of 130 m (430 ft) and duration of 275 seconds has been recorded.


Breeding

The African penguin is monogamous; it breeds in colonies and pairs return to the same site each year. It has an extended breeding season, with nesting usually peaking from March to May in South Africa and November to December in Namibia. A clutch of two eggs is laid either in burrows burrowed in guano or nests in the sand under boulders or bushes. Incubation is undertaken equally by both parents for around 40 days. At least one parent guards the chicks for about one month, whereafter the chicks join a crèche with other chicks and both parents spend most of the day foraging in the sea.
Chicks fledge at 60 to 130 days, the timing depending on environmental factors such as the quality and availability of food. The fledged chicks then go to sea on their own, where they spend the next one to nearly two years. They then return to their natal colony to moult into adult plumage.
When penguins moult, they are unable to forage in the sea as their new feathers are not yet waterproof. Therefore, they fast over the entire moulting period. African penguins typically take around three weeks to moult and lose about half of their body weight by using up their fat reserves in the process.
Breeding success depends strongly on local prey abundance. Birds at the Robben island colony were found lay larger eggs, breed earlier, and produce more fledglings when food availability was high. 
African penguins spend most of their lives at sea until it comes time for them to lay their eggs. Females remain fertile for about 10 years. Due to high predation on the mainland, African penguins will seek protection on offshore islands, where they are safer from larger mammals and natural challenges. These penguins usually breed during the winter when temperatures are cooler. African penguins often will abandon their eggs if they become overheated in the hot sun and abandoned eggs never survive the heat. The eggs are three to four times bigger than chicken eggs. Ideally, the eggs are incubated in a burrow dug into the guano layer (which provides suitable temperature regulation), but the widespread human removal of guano deposits has rendered this type of nest unfeasible in many colonies. Humans mined guano for fertilizer. To compensate, penguins burrow holes in the sand, nest under rocks or bushes or make use of nest boxes if they are provided. The penguins spend three weeks on land caring for their offspring, after which chicks may be left alone during the day while the parents forage. The chicks are frequently killed by predators or succumb to the hot sun. Parents usually feed hatchlings during dusk or dawn.
Artificial nest boxes providing shelter from temperature extremes and predators were trialled on Robben Island, and birds using them were found to have higher chick survival rates than those using open nests.   
In 2015, when foraging conditions were favourable, more male than female African penguin chicks were produced in the colony on Bird Island. Male chicks also had higher growth rates and fledging mass and therefore may have higher post-fledging survival than females. This, coupled with higher adult female mortality in this species, may result in a male-biased adult sex ratio and may indicate that conservation strategies benefiting female African penguins may be necessary.


Predation
The average lifespan of an African penguin is 10 to around 25 years in the wild and up to 34 in captivity.
The primary predators of African penguins at sea include sharks and fur seals. Predators of nesting penguins and their chicks include kelp gulls, Cape genets, mongooses, caracals and domestic cats and dogs. Mortality from terrestrial predators is higher if penguins are forced to breed in the open, in the absence of suitable burrows or nest boxes.


Threats and conservation


Historical exploitation
African penguin eggs were considered a delicacy and were still being eaten and collected for sale as recently as the 1970s. In the 1950s, they were being collected from Dassen Island and sold in nearby towns. In 1953, 12,000 eggs were collected. In the late 1950s, some French chefs expressed interest in recipes including African penguin eggs collected from the islands off the west coast of South Africa and placed annual orders for small quantities. In the mid-1960s, eggs were collected in the thousands and sold by the dozen, with each customer limited to two dozen eggs in total.
The practice of collecting African penguin eggs involved smashing those found a few days before a collecting effort to ensure that only freshly laid eggs were sold. This added to the drastic decline of the African penguin population around the Cape coast, a decline which was hastened by the removal of guano from islands for use as fertiliser, eliminating the burrowing material used by penguins.


Oil spills
Penguins remain susceptible to pollution of their habitat by petrochemicals from spills, shipwrecks and cleaning of tankers while at sea. Accounts of African penguins impacted by oil date back to the 1930s. African penguins' exposure to oil spills is both chronic (higher frequency small discharges of oil at sea) and acute (rare maritime disasters where large volumes of oil are released in a single event). Penguins of many species have been impacted by oil spills across the southern hemisphere.
In 1948, the tanker Esso Wheeling sank, subsequently oiling and killing thousands of penguins of the Dyer Island colony. In 1953, dead penguins were among a range of dead birds, fish and other marine life that washed ashore after the tanker Sliedrecht was holed and spilled oil near Table Bay. In 1971, the SS Wafra oil spill impacted the African penguin colony of Dyer Island. In 1972, oil spilt following the Oswego-Guardian and Texanita collision oiled roughly 500 penguins. In 1975, newspapers reported that oil pollution from shipwrecks and the pumping of bilges at sea had killed tens of thousands of African penguins. At the time, the Dassen Island colony was being passed by 650 oil tankers each month because the Suez Canal had become blocked with wrecked vessels, thus increasing maritime traffic past the Cape of Good Hope.
In 1979, an oil spill prompted the collection and treatment of 150 African penguins from St. Croix Island near Port Elizabeth. The animals were later released at Robben Island and four of them promptly swam back to St. Croix Island, surprising scientists.
In 1983, the exposure of penguins of Dassen Island to the oil slick from the Castillo de Bellver was also a topic of concern given the penguins' conservation status at the time, but owing to the prevailing wind and current, only gannets were oiled.


1994 MV Apollo Sea disaster
African penguin casualties were significant following the sinking of the MV Apollo Sea and a subsequent oil slick in 1994. 10,000 penguins were collected and cleaned, of which less than half survived.


2000 MV Treasure crisis
Disaster struck on 23 June 2000, when the iron ore tanker MV Treasure sank between Robben Island and Dassen Island, South Africa. It released 400–1,000 tonnes (390–980 long tons; 440–1,100 short tons) of fuel oil, causing an unprecedented coastal bird crisis and oiling 19,000 adult penguins at the height of the best breeding season on record for this vulnerable species. The oiled birds were brought to an abandoned train repair warehouse in Cape Town to be cared for. An additional 19,500 un-oiled penguins were removed from Dassen Island and other areas before they became oiled and were released about 800 kilometres east of Cape Town. This gave workers enough time to clean up the oiled waters and shores before the birds could complete their long swim home (which took the penguins between one and three weeks). Some of the penguins were named and radio-tracked as they swam back to their breeding grounds. Tens of thousands of volunteers helped with the rescue and rehabilitation process, which was overseen by the International Fund for Animal Welfare (IFAW) and the South African Foundation for the Conservation of Coastal Birds (SANCCOB) and took more than three months to complete. This was the largest animal rescue event in history; more than 91% of the penguins were successfully rehabilitated and released – an amazing feat that could not have been accomplished without such a tremendous international response.
Due to the positive outcome of African penguins being raised in captivity after tragedies such as the Treasure oil spill, the species is considered a good "candidate for a captive-breeding programme which aims to release offspring into the wild"; however, worry about the spread of new strains of avian malaria is a major concern in the situation.
Bringing the birds inland led to the exposure of penguins to parasites and disease vectors such as mosquitoes carrying avian malaria, which has caused 27% of the rehabilitated penguin deaths annually.


2016 & 2019 Port of Ngqura
Small-scale oil spills (of less than 400 litres (110 US gal)) have occurred at the Port of Ngqura since bunkering activities started there in 2016. Bunkering is a ship refuelling process that can result in oil spills and oil slicks entering the water. Hundreds of African penguins have been harmed following these spills due to the port's close proximity to penguin rookeries on St. Croix Island and seabird habitat on neighbouring Jahleel and Brenton Islands.


Competition with fisheries
The population collapse in the 2000s appears to be largely due to the decrease of food availability, principally sardines and anchovy stocks. Commercial fisheries of these fish have forced these penguins to search for prey farther offshore, as well as having to switch to eating less nutritious prey. Restricting commercial fishing near colony sites such as Robben Island for short periods (3 years) was shown to markedly improve penguin breeding success. Longer closure periods and closures near other colonies are being evaluated.
Fishing pressure and climate variability have engendered major ecological changes in the Benguela system. African penguins are highly sensitive to any type of decline in sardine and anchovies. Current food availability is considered to now limit colonies to relatively small sizes. 


Cape fur seal predation
Sustained conservation efforts to increase the number of Cape fur seals are thought to have contributed to the decline of African penguin populations. In Namibia, breeding colonies have been decimated by the predation of "rogue" Cape fur seals on African penguins. Penguins on Dyer Island in South Africa face the same threat. Conservationists consider culling of seals near breeding colonies as an effective recourse. Observational results from Mercury Island indicate that seal culling led to an increase in the population of penguins.


Conservation status
The African penguin is one of the species to which the African-Eurasian Waterbird Agreement (AEWA) applies. In September 2010, it was listed as endangered under the US Endangered Species Act. As of 2024, the African penguin is listed as critically endangered on the IUCN Red List, with the remaining mature individuals around 19,800 birds in a declining population.


Mediation efforts
Many organisations such as SANCCOB, Dyer Island Conservation Trust, SAMREC, The National Aviary in Pittsburgh, and Raggy Charters with the Penguin Research Fund in Port Elizabeth are working to halt the decline of the African penguin. Measures include: monitoring population trends, hand-rearing and releasing abandoned chicks, establishing artificial nests and proclaiming marine reserves in which fishing is prohibited. Some colonies (such as on Dyer Island) are suspected to be under heavy pressure from predation by Cape fur seals and may benefit from the culling of individual problem animals, which has been found effective (although requiring a large amount of management effort) in trials.
Established in 1968, SANCCOB is currently the only organisation mandated by the South African government to respond to crises involving seabirds along South Africa's coastline and is internationally recognised for the role it played during the MV Treasure oil spill. A modelling exercise conducted in 2003 by the University of Cape Town's FitzPatrick Institute of African Ornithology found that rehabilitating oiled African penguins has resulted in the current population being 19% larger than it would have been in the absence of SANCCOB's rehabilitation efforts.
In February 2015, the Dyer Island Conservation Trust opened the African Penguin and Seabird Sanctuary (APSS) in Gansbaai, South Africa. The centre was opened by then-Department of Tourism minister Derek Hanekom and will serve as a hub for seabird research carried out by the Dyer Island Conservation Trust. The centre will also run local education projects, host international marine volunteers and seek to improve seabird handling techniques and rehabilitation protocols.


Captivity

African penguins are a commonly seen species in zoos across the world. Because they do not require particularly low temperatures, they are often kept in outside enclosures. They adapt fairly well to this captive environment and are rather easy to breed compared to other species of the family. In Europe, the breeding programme EAZA is regulated by Artis Royal Zoo in the Netherlands, whilst in the United States the SSP program is cooperatively managed by the AZA. The idea is to create a backup captive population, as well as to aid in the conservation of the population in its natural habitat. Between 2010 and 2013, American zoos spent $300,000 on in situ (wild population) conservation.

The '''African penguin''' ('''''Spheniscus demersus'''''), also known as '''Cape penguin''' or '''South African penguin''', is a species of [[penguin]] confined to southern [[Africa]]n waters. It is the only penguin found in the [[Old World]]. Like all penguins, it is [[Flightless bird|flightless]], with a streamlined body and wings stiffened and flattened into [[Flipper (anatomy)|flippers]] for a marine habitat. Adults weigh an average of {{cvt|2.2|–|3.5|kg|lb}} and are {{cvt|60|–|70|cm|in}} tall. The species has distinctive pink patches of skin above the eyes and a black facial mask. The body's upper parts are black and sharply delineated from the white underparts, which are spotted and marked with a black band.

The African penguin is a [[Seabird#Pursuit diving|pursuit diver]] and feeds primarily on fish and squid. Once extremely numerous, the African penguin is now the rarest species of penguin, classified as [[critically endangered]], with its population declining rapidly due to a combination of several threats, such as habitat loss, overfishing and [[climate change]]. It is a charismatic species and is popular with tourists. Other [[vernacular name]]s of the species include '''black-footed penguin''' and '''jackass penguin''', due to the species' loud, [[donkey]]-like noise<ref name="Favaro et al">{{cite journal|last1=Favaro|first1=Livio|last2=Ozella|first2=Laura|last3=Pessani|first3=Daniela|last4=Pavan|first4=Gianni|title=The Vocal Repertoire of the African Penguin (''Spheniscus demersus''): Structure and Function of Calls|journal=PLOS ONE|date=30 July 2014|volume=9|issue=7|article-number=e103460|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0103460|pmid=25076136|pmc=4116197|bibcode=2014PLoSO...9j3460F|doi-access=free}}</ref> (although several related species of South American penguins produce the same sound). They can be found along the coast of [[South Africa]] and [[Namibia]].

==Description==
[[File:Spheniscus demersus 01.JPG|thumb|left|African penguin diving in [[Siam Center]], [[Bangkok]], [[Thailand]]]]
[[File:African Penguin Skeleton.jpg|thumb|African penguin skeleton at the [[Museum of Osteology]] in [[Oklahoma City]], [[Oklahoma]], US]]
African penguins grow to {{convert|60|–|70|cm|in|abbr=on}} tall and weigh between {{convert|2.2|–|3.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Sinclair, Ian; Hockey, Phil; Tarboton, Warwick; Ryan, Peter 2011 22">{{cite book|author1=Sinclair, Ian|author2=Hockey, Phil|author3=Tarboton, Warwick|author4=Ryan, Peter|title=Sasol Birds Of South Africa|year=2011|publisher=Struik|isbn=978-1-77007-925-0|page=22}}</ref> The beak length of the African penguin varies, usually growing between {{cvt|4.8|–|6.5|cm}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Campbell |first1=Kate J |last2=Farah |first2=Danielle |last3=Collins |first3=Sarah |last4=Parsons |first4=Nola J |title=Sex determination of African Penguins Spheniscus demersus using bill measurements: method comparisons and implications for use |journal=Ostrich |date=2 January 2016 |volume=87 |issue=1 |pages=47–55 |doi=10.2989/00306525.2015.1108371 |bibcode=2016Ostri..87...47C |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.2989/00306525.2015.1108371|url-access=subscription }}</ref> They have a black stripe and black spots on the chest, the pattern of which is unique to each penguin, like human fingerprints. The sweat glands above the eyes cool the birds' blood and as the temperature rises, increased blood flow causes the glands to get pinker.<ref name="Wildlife Monthly">{{cite web|url=http://wildlifemonthly.blogspot.com/2012_02_01_archive.html|author=Mahard, Tyler|year=2012|title=The Black-footed Penguin ''Spheniscus demersus''|publisher=Wildlife Monthly|access-date=2012-11-20}}</ref> This species exhibits slight [[sexual dimorphism]]; the males are slightly larger than the females and have longer beaks.<ref name="Dyer Island Conservation Trust">{{cite web|url=http://www.dict.org.za/penguin.php|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091026082526/http://www.dict.org.za/penguin.php|archive-date=2009-10-26|title=African Penguin (''Spheniscus demersus'')|publisher=Dyer Island Conservation Trust}}</ref> Juveniles do not possess the bold, delineated markings of the adult, but instead have dark upperparts that vary from greyish-blue to brown; the pale underparts lack both spots and the band. The beak is more pointed than that of the [[Humboldt penguin]]. The African penguin's colouring is a form of protective colouration known as [[countershading]]. The white undersides of the birds are difficult to spot by predators under the water and the penguins' black backs blend in with the water when viewed from above. African penguins possess supraorbital salt glands located above the eyes that allow them to excrete excess salt from ingesting seawater, an adaptation that helps maintain osmotic balance while foraging at sea.<ref>{{cite web |title=African Penguin |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22697810/157423972 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |access-date=19 February 2026}}</ref>

African penguins resemble and are related to the Humboldt, [[Magellanic penguin|Magellanic]] and [[Galapagos penguin|Galápagos]] penguins.<ref name="azpenguin">{{cite web|author=a-z animals|title=The African Penguin|publisher=a-z animals|access-date=2013-07-09|url=http://a-z-animals.com/animals/african-penguin/}}</ref> African penguins have a very recognisable appearance, with a thick band of black that is in the shape of an upside-down horseshoe. They have black feet and black spots that vary in size and shape between individuals. Magellanic penguins share a similar bar marking that often confuses the two; the Magellanic has a double bar on the throat and chest, whereas the African has a single bar. These penguins are sometimes called "jackass penguins", which comes from the loud braying noises they make.

==Distribution==
The African penguin is found on the southern and southwestern coast of Africa, living in colonies on 27 islands between Namibia and [[Algoa Bay]], near [[Port Elizabeth]], South Africa.<ref name="IUCN">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2020 |title=''Spheniscus demersus'' |volume=2020 |article-number=e.T22697810A157423361 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T22697810A157423361.en |access-date=11 September 2023}}</ref> It is the only penguin species that breeds in Africa, and its presence gave name to the [[Penguin Islands]].
[[Image:African penguins with chicks.jpg|thumb|left|Two adults with chicks at the [[Boulders Beach]] colony in South Africa]]

Two colonies were established by penguins in the 1980s on the mainland near Cape Town, namely [[Boulders Beach]] near [[Simon's Town]] and Stony Point in [[Betty's Bay]]. Mainland colonies likely became possible only in recent times due to the reduction of predator numbers, although the Betty's Bay colony has been attacked by [[leopard]]s.<ref name="Betty's Bay">{{cite web|url=http://www.bettysbay.info/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=24&Itemid=26|title=The African Penguin|publisher=Bettysbay|date=2010-04-08|access-date=2012-03-30|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20120530051910/http://www.bettysbay.info/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=24&Itemid=26|archive-date= 2012-05-30}}</ref><ref name="Cape Nature">{{cite web |url= http://www.capenature.co.za/%E2%80%8Bcapenature-increases-%E2%80%8B%E2%80%8Bprotection-predators-stony-point/|title=CapeNature increases protection from predators at Stony Point|publisher=CapeNature|access-date=2020-07-06|archive-date=2018-08-07|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20180807094902/https://www.capenature.co.za/%e2%80%8bcapenature-increases-%e2%80%8b%e2%80%8bprotection-predators-stony-point/ }}</ref> The only other mainland colony is in Namibia, but it is not known when it was established.

Boulders Beach is a tourist attraction due to the beach, swimming, and the penguins themselves.<ref name="SANParks">{{cite web|url=http://www.sanparks.org/parks/table_mountain/tourism/attractions.php#boulders|title=Table Mountain National Park|publisher=SANParks|access-date=2012-03-30}}</ref><ref name="GoAfrica">{{cite web|url=http://goafrica.about.com/od/capetownatravelguide/a/bouldersbeach.htm|title=Boulders Beach, Swimming with Penguins – Swimming with Penguins in South Africa|publisher=Goafrica|date=2010-06-14|access-date=2012-03-30|archive-date=2013-12-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203045011/http://goafrica.about.com/od/capetownatravelguide/a/bouldersbeach.htm}}</ref> The penguins will allow people to approach them as close as a meter.

Breeding populations of African penguins are being kept in numerous zoos worldwide. No colonies are known outside the southwestern coast of Africa, although [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrants]] (mostly juveniles) may occasionally be sighted beyond the normal range.

=== Population ===
Roughly 4 million African penguins existed at the beginning of the 19th century. Of the 1.5 million African penguins estimated in 1910, only some 10% remained at the end of the 20th century. African penguin populations, which breed in Namibia and South Africa, have declined by 95% since pre-industrial times.<ref name=seaturtles>{{cite web|url=http://www.seaturtles.org/article.php?id=1800|title=Vanishing African Penguin, Threatened by Climate Change and Fishing, Wins Protections|publisher=seaturtles.org|last=Kilduff|first=Catherine|date=2010-09-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203072203/http://www.seaturtles.org/article.php?id=1800|archive-date=2013-12-03}}</ref>

Today, breeding is largely restricted to 24 islands from Namibia to Algoa Bay, South Africa,<ref name="Atlanta">{{Cite news|date=2006-11-17|title=Know your Georgia Aquarium penguins (2006)|pages=G6|work=The Atlanta Constitution|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561897/know-your-georgia-aquarium-penguins/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> with the Boulders Beach colony being an exception to this rule. The total population fell to approximately 150,000–180,000 in 2000.<ref name="York">{{Cite news|date=2000-10-12|title=Penguins begin swim home (Treasure, 2000)|page=6|work=York Daily Record|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561727/penguins-begin-swim-home-treasure/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref><ref name="Calgary">{{Cite news|date=2000-07-21|title=Penguins elude oil spill but fishermen open fire, South Africa (2000)|page=12|work=Calgary Herald|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/54568076/penguins-elude-oil-spill-but-fishermen/|access-date=2020-07-02}}</ref> Of those, 56,000 belonged to the [[Dassen Island]] colony and 14,000 to the [[Robben Island]] colony.<ref name="Tampa Times">{{Cite news|date=2000-07-08|title=Flight of the African penguin (2000)|page=2|work=Tampa Bay Times|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561218/flight-of-the-african-penguin-2000/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> The colony at [[Gansbaai#Dyer Island, Geyser Rock and Shark Alley|Dyer Island]] in South Africa fell from 46,000 in the early 1970s to 3,000 in 2008.<ref>{{Cite news|date=2009-03-30|title=Experiments seek to house declining South African penguins (2009)|pages=A6|work=The Boston Globe|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561597/experiments-seek-to-house-declining/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref>

In 2008, 5,000 breeding pairs were estimated to live in Namibia.

In 2010, the total African penguin population was estimated at 55,000. At the rate of decline seen from 2000 to 2010, the African penguin was expected to be extinct in the wild by 2026.<ref name="Globalpost">{{cite web|url=http://www.globalpost.com/dispatch/news/regions/africa/south-africa/110617/african-penguins-endangered-species-cape-town|title=African Penguin; Endangered; Cape Town|publisher=Globalpost.com|date=2011-06-19|access-date=2012-03-30}}</ref>

In 2012, about 18,700 breeding pairs were estimated to live in South Africa, with the majority on [[St. Croix Island (Algoa Bay)|St. Croix Island]] in [[Algoa Bay]].<ref name="IUCN" /><ref>Makhado, A. B., B. N. Dyer, R. Fox, D. Geldenhuys, L. Pichegru, R. M. Randall, R .B. Sherley, L. Upfold, J. Visagie., L. J. Waller, P. A. Whittington, and R. J .M. Crawford. 2013. Estimates of numbers of twelve seabird species breeding in South Africa, updated to include 2012. Branch Oceans and Coasts, Department of Environmental Affairs, Cape Town.</ref>

The total breeding population across both South Africa and Namibia fell to about 20,850 pairs in 2019<ref name="bioRxiv">{{Cite journal|last1=Sherley|first1=R. B.|last2=Crawford|first2=R. J.|last3=de Blocq|first3=A. D.|last4=Dyer|first4=B. M.|last5=Geldenhuys|first5=D.|last6=Hagen|first6=C.|last7=Kemper|first7=J.|last8=Makhado|first8=A. B.|last9=Pichegru|first9=L.|last10=Upfold|first10=L.|last11=Visagie|first11=J.|first12=Lauren J.|last12=Waller|first13=Henning|last13=Winker|title=The conservation status and population decline of the African penguin deconstructed in space and time |journal=Ecology and Evolution |date=2020 |volume=10 |issue=15 |pages=8506–8516|doi=10.1002/ece3.6554 |bibcode=2020EcoEv..10.8506S |biorxiv=10.1101/2020.01.15.907485}}</ref> and further declined to below 10,000 pairs in 2023. At this point the species was reclassified as [[critically endangered]], with the suggestion that it would become [[functionally extinct]] in about 2035 if the current trajectory continued.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://sanccob.co.za/news/african-penguin-newly-classified-as-critically-endangered-as-breeding-pairs-fall-below-10000/|title=African Penguin newly classified as 'critically endangered' as breeding pairs fall below 10,000|date=28 October 2024 |publisher=SANCOBB|access-date=19 March 2025}}</ref> Despite expert recommendations for immediate and wide-ranging closures of breeding areas to fishing,<ref>{{cite report|last1=Punt|first1=AE|last2=Furness|first2=RW|last3=Parma|first3=A.M|last4=Plagányi-Lloyd|first4=E|last5=Sanchirico|first5=JN|last6=Trathan|first6=P.|year=2023|title=Report of the international review panel regarding fishing closures adjacent to South Africa's African penguin breeding colonies and declines in the penguin population. Prepared for the Department of Forestry, Fisheries and the Environment (DFFE)|publisher=DFFE|location=Pretoria, South Africa|url=https://biodiversitylaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/International-Panel-Report-August-2023.pdf}}</ref> the South African government chose to merely retain a number of previously instituted trial closures that had been shown to be largely ineffective. In response, SANCOBB and [[BirdLife South Africa]] together with the [[Biodiversity Law Centre]] launched a landmark litigation to revise the decision, based on invoking the government's constitutional obligation to prevent extinction of an endangered species.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://biodiversitylaw.org/breaking-landmark-litigation-launched-to-protect-the-african-penguin-from-extinction/|title=Landmark litigation to protect the African Penguin from extinction|date=20 March 2024 |publisher=Biodiversity Law Centre|access-date=19 March 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.iol.co.za/news/crime-and-courts/legal-battle-to-save-african-penguin-intensifies-with-new-court-filings-11579b29-e22d-460b-816a-d730633b50a9|title=Legal battle to save African penguin intensifies with new court filings|date=23 October 1970 |publisher=IOL|access-date=19 March 2025}}</ref> On 18 March 2025, prior to the court hearing, a settlement was reached in which the Ministry set aside its earlier decision and decreed the establishment of a set of larger and full-time no-fishing zones around six key breeding areas.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c5y0xl8gp14o|title=Last-minute deal to protect African penguins from extinction|author = Gabby Colenso|publisher=BBC News|date=18 March 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://sanccob.co.za/news/high-court-victory-for-the-critically-endangered-african-penguin/|title=High Court victory for the Critically Endangered African Penguin|date=18 March 2025 |publisher=SANCOBB|access-date=19 March 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://biodiversitylaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/20250318-BLSA-Minister-Order.pdf|title=Order of the High Court of South Africa, 18 March 2025|access-date=19 March 2025}}</ref>

==Behaviour==

===Diet===
African penguins forage in the open sea, where they feed on [[pelagic fish]] such as [[sardines]] (including the [[Sardinops|blue pilchard]]), [[South African mullet]], [[Cape horse mackerel]]s, [[Etrumeus sadina|red-eye round herring]]s<ref name="animaldiversity.org">{{cite web | url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Spheniscus_demersus/ | title=Spheniscus demersus (Jackass penguin) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]] }}</ref> and [[Anchovy|anchovies]] (specifically the [[European anchovy]] and the [[Southern African anchovy]])<ref name="ARKive">{{cite web|url=http://www.arkive.org/african-penguin/spheniscus-demersus/#text=Biology|title=African penguin videos, photos and facts – Spheniscus demersus|publisher=ARKive|access-date=2012-03-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120322050835/http://www.arkive.org/african-penguin/spheniscus-demersus/#text=Biology|archive-date=2012-03-22}}</ref><ref name="animaldiversity.org"/> and marine invertebrates such as [[squid]]s (''[[Loligo reynaudii|Loligo reynaudi]])'' and small [[crustacean]]s, primarily [[krill]]s, [[shrimp]]s and ''Pterygosquilla armata''.<ref name="diet">{{cite web|url=http://www.simonstown.com/tourism/penguins/penguins.htm|title=The African Penguin Simons Town|publisher=Simonstown.com|access-date=2012-03-30|archive-date=2013-11-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131102120851/http://simonstown.com/tourism/penguins/penguins.htm}}</ref><ref name="Williams1995">{{cite book |last=Williams |first=Tony D. |title=The Penguins: Spheniscidae |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1995 |pages=241-242}}</ref> Penguins normally swim within {{cvt|20|km|mi}} of the shore.<ref name="Sinclair, Ian; Hockey, Phil; Tarboton, Warwick; Ryan, Peter 2011 22"/> A penguin may consume up to {{Convert|540|g|lb|abbr=}} of prey every day,<ref name=crawford1991>{{cite journal|last1=Crawford|first1=R. J. M.|last2=Ryan|first2=P. G.|last3=Williams|first3=A. J.|year=1991|title=Seabird consumption and production in the Benguela and western Agulhas ecosystems|journal=South African Journal of Marine Science|volume=11|issue=1|pages=357–375|doi=10.2989/025776191784287709 |bibcode=1991SAJMS..11..357C }}</ref> but this may increase to over {{Cvt|1|kg|lb|abbr=}} when raising older chicks.<ref name="diet" />

Due to the marked decline of sardines in the waters near its habitat, African penguins' diet has shifted towards anchovies to some extent, although available sardine [[Biomass (ecology)|biomass]] is still a notable determinant of penguin population development and breeding success. While a diet of anchovies appears to be generally sufficient for the penguins, it is not ideal due to anchovies' lower concentrations of [[fat]] and [[protein]]. The species' diet changes throughout the year; as in many [[Seabird|seabirds]], it is believed that the interaction of diet choice and breeding success helps the penguins maintain their population size. Although parent penguins are protective of their chicks, they will not incur nutritional deficits themselves if food is scarce and hunting requires a greater time or energy commitment. This may lead to higher rates of brood loss under poor food conditions.

When foraging, African penguins carry out dives that reach an average depth of {{Cvt|25|m|ft|abbr=}} and last for 69 seconds, although a maximum depth of {{Cvt|130|m|ft|abbr=}} and duration of 275 seconds has been recorded.<ref name="Penguiness">{{cite journal|author1=Ropert-Coudert Y |author2=Kato A |author3=Robbins A |author4=Humphries GRW|title=African Penguin (''Spheniscus demersus'')|website=The Penguiness book, version 3.0|url=http://www.penguiness.net|date=October 2018|doi=10.13140/RG.2.2.32289.66406|publisher=Unpublished}}</ref>

===Breeding===
[[File:Spheniscus demersus MWNH 0522.JPG|thumb|Egg from the collection of [[Museum Wiesbaden]], [[Wiesbaden]], [[Hesse]], Germany]]
[[File:Bruthöhlen Brillenpinguin.jpg|thumb|Nesting burrows of the African penguin, [[Boulders Beach]] (2017)]]
The African penguin is [[monogamous]]; it breeds in colonies and pairs return to the same site each year. It has an extended breeding season, with nesting usually peaking from March to May in South Africa and November to December in Namibia.<ref name="ARKive"/> A clutch of two eggs is laid either in burrows burrowed in [[guano]] or nests in the sand under boulders or bushes. Incubation is undertaken equally by both parents for around 40 days. At least one parent guards the chicks for about one month, whereafter the chicks join a [[Crèche (zoology)|crèche]] with other chicks and both parents spend most of the day foraging in the sea.

Chicks [[fledge]] at 60 to 130 days, the timing depending on environmental factors such as the quality and availability of food. The fledged chicks then go to sea on their own, where they spend the next one to nearly two years. They then return to their natal colony to [[Moulting|moult]] into adult plumage.<ref name=wolfardt>{{cite journal|last1=Wolfaardt|first1=A. C.|last2=Underhill|first2=L. G.|last3=Visagie|first3=J.|year=2009|title=Breeding and moult phenology of African penguins Spheniscus demersus at Dassen Island|journal=African Journal of Marine Science|volume=31|issue=2|pages=119–132|doi=10.2989/AJMS.2009.31.2.1.873|bibcode=2009AfJMS..31..119W |s2cid=85264159 }}</ref>

When penguins moult, they are unable to forage in the sea as their new feathers are not yet waterproof. Therefore, they fast over the entire moulting period.<ref name=wolfardt/> African penguins typically take around three weeks to moult and lose about half of their body weight by using up their fat reserves in the process.<ref name="Toronto Zoo">{{cite web|url=http://www.torontozoo.com/animals/African%20Penguin|title=African Penguin|website=Toronto Zoo|access-date=5 July 2020}}</ref>

Breeding success depends strongly on local prey abundance. Birds at the Robben island colony were found lay larger eggs, breed earlier, and produce more fledglings when food availability was high.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Crawford |first1=Robert J.M. |last2=Barham |first2=Peter J. |last3=Underhill |first3=Les G. |last4=Shannon |first4=Lynne J. |last5=Coetzee |first5=Janet C. |last6=Dyer |first6=Bruce M. |last7=Leshoro |first7=T. Mario |last8=Upfold |first8=Leshia |date=2006|title=The influence of food availability on breeding success of African penguins Spheniscus demersus at Robben Island, South Africa |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S000632070600142X |journal=Biological Conservation |language=en |volume=132 |issue=1 |pages=119–125 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2006.03.019 |bibcode=2006BCons.132..119C }}</ref> 

African penguins spend most of their lives at sea until it comes time for them to lay their eggs. Females remain fertile for about 10 years. Due to high predation on the mainland, African penguins will seek protection on offshore islands, where they are safer from larger mammals and natural challenges. These penguins usually breed during the winter when temperatures are cooler. African penguins often will abandon their eggs if they become overheated in the hot sun and abandoned eggs never survive the heat. The eggs are three to four times bigger than chicken eggs. Ideally, the eggs are incubated in a burrow dug into the [[guano]] layer (which provides suitable temperature regulation), but the widespread human removal of guano deposits has rendered this type of nest unfeasible in many colonies. Humans mined guano for fertilizer. To compensate, penguins burrow holes in the sand, nest under rocks or bushes or make use of nest boxes if they are provided. The penguins spend three weeks on land caring for their offspring, after which chicks may be left alone during the day while the parents forage. The chicks are frequently killed by predators or succumb to the hot sun. Parents usually feed hatchlings during dusk or dawn.

Artificial nest boxes providing shelter from temperature extremes and predators were trialled on Robben Island, and birds using them were found to have higher chick survival rates than those using open nests.<ref name=nests>{{Cite journal |last1=Sherley |first1=Richard B. |last2=Barham |first2=Barbara J. |last3=Barham |first3=Peter J. |last4=Leshoro |first4=T. Mario |last5=Underhill |first5=Les G. |date=2012|title=Artificial nests enhance the breeding productivity of African Penguins ( Spheniscus demersus ) on Robben Island, South Africa |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1071/MU11055 |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |language=en |volume=112 |issue=2 |pages=97–106 |doi=10.1071/MU11055 |bibcode=2012EmuAO.112...97S |issn=0158-4197}}</ref>   

In 2015, when foraging conditions were favourable, more male than female African penguin chicks were produced in the colony on Bird Island. Male chicks also had higher growth rates and fledging mass and therefore may have higher post-fledging survival than females. This, coupled with higher adult female mortality in this species, may result in a male-biased adult sex ratio and may indicate that conservation strategies benefiting female African penguins may be necessary.<ref name="Ibis">{{cite journal|author1=Spelt, A.|author2=Pichegru, L.|year=2017|title=Sex allocation and sex-specific parental investment in an endangered seabird|journal=Ibis|volume=159|pages=272–284|doi=10.1111/ibi.12457|issue=2}}</ref>

===Predation===
The average lifespan of an African penguin is 10 to around 25 years in the wild and up to 34 in captivity.<ref name="ADW">{{cite web|url=http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/site/accounts/information/Spheniscus_demersus.html|title=ADW: Spheniscus demersus: Information|publisher=Animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu|date=2010-02-01|access-date=2012-03-30}}</ref><ref name="NYT">{{cite web|url= https://www.nytimes.com/2025/03/16/science/penguin-retirement-home-aquarium.html |title=NYT: Penguin Retirement Home|work=[[The New York Times]]|date=2025-03-16|access-date=2025-03-21}}</ref>

The primary predators of African penguins at sea include [[shark]]s and [[fur seal]]s. Predators of nesting penguins and their chicks include [[kelp gull]]s, [[Cape genet]]s, [[mongoose]]s, [[caracal]]s and domestic [[cat]]s and [[dog]]s.<ref name="Ostrich">{{cite journal|last1=Whittington|first1=P. A.|last2=Hofmeyr|first2=J. H.|last3=Cooper|first3=J.|year=1996|title=Establishment, growth and conservation of a mainland colony of Jackass Penguins ''Spheniscus demersus'' at Stony Point, Betty's Bay, South Africa|doi=10.1080/00306525.1996.9639700|journal=Ostrich|volume=67|issue=3–4|pages=144–150|bibcode=1996Ostri..67..144W }}</ref><ref name="neaq">{{cite web|url=http://www.neaq.org/animals_and_exhibits/animals/african_penguin/index.php|title=African penguin|publisher=Neaq.org|access-date=2012-03-30|archive-date=2012-03-25|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120325143545/http://www.neaq.org/animals_and_exhibits/animals/african_penguin/index.php}}</ref> Mortality from terrestrial predators is higher if penguins are forced to breed in the open, in the absence of suitable burrows or nest boxes.

==Threats and conservation==
[[Image:AfricanPenguinNEAq.jpg|thumb|African penguin at the [[New England Aquarium]], [[Boston]], [[Massachusetts]], United States]]
[[File:Spheniscus demersus 2010.ogv|thumb|(video) African penguin swims in an aquarium in [[Tokyo]], Japan]]

===Historical exploitation===
African penguin eggs were considered a delicacy and were still being eaten and collected for sale as recently as the 1970s. In the 1950s, they were being collected from [[Dassen Island]] and sold in nearby towns.<ref name="Pantagraph">{{Cite news|date=1955-01-09|title=Dassen Island African penguins (1955)|page=10|work=The Pantagraph|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50560484/dassen-island-african-penguins-1955/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> In 1953, 12,000 eggs were collected.<ref name="Sun-bulletin">{{Cite news|date=1954-10-23|title=Penguin playmates & egg exports, Dassen Island (1954)|page=4|work=Press and Sun-Bulletin|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50560421/penguin-playmates-egg-exports-dassen/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> In the late 1950s, some French chefs expressed interest in recipes including African penguin eggs collected from the islands off the west coast of South Africa and placed annual orders for small quantities.<ref name="Racine">{{Cite news|date=1959-08-09|title=French Gourmet Finds Penguin Egg Recipes (1959)|page=17|work=The Racine Journal-Times Sunday Bulletin|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50557919/french-gourmet-finds-penguin-egg/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref><ref name="Troy Record">{{Cite news|date=1956-07-23|title=Penguin-egg eating may spread (1959)|page=14|work=The Troy Record|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50558213/penguin-egg-eating-may-spread-1959/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref><ref name="Oklahoman">{{Cite news|date=1959-05-24|title=Penguin eggs sent to French eaters (1959)|page=48|work=The Daily Oklahoman|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50559867/penguin-eggs-sent-to-french-eaters/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> In the mid-1960s, eggs were collected in the thousands and sold by the dozen,<ref name="Arizona">{{Cite news|date=1965-05-30|title=Penguins' Eggs Again To Grace Gourmets' Tables (1965)|page=32|work=Arizona Daily Star|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561011/penguins-eggs-again-to-grace-gourmets/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> with each customer limited to two dozen eggs in total.<ref name="Sioux">{{Cite news|date=1965-05-09|title=Must Ration Penguin Eggs at $2.10 a Dozen (1965)|page=49|work=Sioux City Journal|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561159/must-ration-penguin-eggs-at-210-a/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref>

The practice of collecting African penguin eggs involved smashing those found a few days before a collecting effort to ensure that only freshly laid eggs were sold. This added to the drastic decline of the African penguin population around the Cape coast, a decline which was hastened by the removal of [[guano]] from islands for use as fertiliser, eliminating the burrowing material used by penguins.<ref name="shannon">{{cite journal|last1=Shannon|first1=L. J.|last2=Crawford|first2=R. J. M.|year=1999|title=Management of the African penguin Spheniscus demersus — insights from modelling|journal=Marine Ornithology|volume=27|pages=119–128 |doi=10.5038/2074-1235.27.1.432 }}</ref>

=== Oil spills ===
Penguins remain susceptible to pollution of their habitat by [[petrochemicals]] from spills, shipwrecks and cleaning of tankers while at sea. Accounts of African [[List of oil spills impacting penguins|penguins impacted by oil]] date back to the 1930s.<ref name="Hartford">{{Cite news|date=1936-08-02|title=Oily penguin, South Africa (1936)|page=70|work=Hartford Courant|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50561469/oily-penguin-south-africa-1936/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> African penguins' exposure to oil spills is both chronic (higher frequency small discharges of oil at sea) and acute (rare maritime disasters where large volumes of oil are released in a single event). Penguins of many species have been [[List of oil spills impacting penguins|impacted by oil spills]] across the southern hemisphere.

In 1948, the tanker ''Esso Wheeling'' sank, subsequently oiling and killing thousands of penguins of the Dyer Island colony.<ref>{{Cite news|date=1949-04-29|title=Oil wrecks penguins of Dyer Island (Esso Wheeling, 1949)|page=22|work=The Morning Call|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/54879646/oil-wrecks-penguins-of-dyer-island/|access-date=2020-07-07}}</ref> In 1953, dead penguins were among a range of dead birds, fish and other marine life that washed ashore after the [[Sliedrecht oil spill|tanker ''Sliedrecht'' was holed and spilled oil]] near [[Table Bay]].<ref name="Newcastle">{{Cite news|date=1953-11-03|title=Ship Oil Kills Fish, Penguins|page=8|work=Newcastle Sun (NSW: 1918-1954)|url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article161508121|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> In 1971, the [[SS Wafra oil spill|SS ''Wafra'' oil spill]] impacted the African penguin colony of Dyer Island. In 1972, oil spilt following the [[Oswego-Guardian–Texanita collision|''Oswego-Guardian'' and ''Texanita'']] collision oiled roughly 500 penguins.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Brief History of Penguin Oiling in South African Waters {{!}} Animal Demography Unit|url=http://www.adu.uct.ac.za/adu/projects/sea-shore-birds/oil-spill/history|access-date=2020-07-07|website=www.adu.uct.ac.za|language=en}}</ref> In 1975, newspapers reported that oil pollution from shipwrecks and the [[Bilge pump|pumping of bilges]] at sea had killed tens of thousands of African penguins. At the time, the Dassen Island colony was being passed by 650 oil tankers each month<ref name="AZ Republic">{{Cite news|date=1975-05-01|title=South Africa's penguins die from oil pollution effects (1975)|page=1|work=Arizona Republic|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50559428/south-africas-penguins-die-from-oil/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref> because the [[Suez Canal]] had become blocked with wrecked vessels, thus increasing maritime traffic past the Cape of Good Hope.<ref name=":0" />

In 1979, an oil spill prompted the collection and treatment of 150 African penguins from St. Croix Island near Port Elizabeth. The animals were later released at Robben Island and four of them promptly swam back to St. Croix Island, surprising scientists.<ref name="Charlotte">{{Cite news|date=1979-09-17|title="Homing" Penguins Travel 500 Miles (1979)|page=4|work=The Charlotte News|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50558328/homing-penguins-travel-500-miles/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref><ref name="DailyNews">{{Cite news|date=1979-09-18|title=Penguins baffle scientists (1979)|page=27|work=The Daily News|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50559316/penguins-baffle-scientists-1979/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref>

In 1983, the exposure of penguins of Dassen Island to the oil slick from the [[Castillo de Bellver oil spill|Castillo de Bellver]] was also a topic of concern given the penguins' conservation status at the time, but owing to the prevailing wind and current, only [[gannets]] were oiled.<ref name="Baytown">{{Cite news|date=1983-08-08|title=Split Supertanker Gets Slow Tow Out To Sea (Castillo de Bellver oil spill, 1983)|page=6|work=The Baytown Sun|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/50557292/split-supertanker-gets-slow-tow-out-to/|access-date=2020-05-09}}</ref>

==== 1994 MV ''Apollo Sea'' disaster ====
African penguin casualties were significant following the sinking of the [[Apollo Sea|MV ''Apollo Sea'']] and a subsequent oil slick in 1994. 10,000 penguins were collected and cleaned, of which less than half survived.<ref name="wolfaardt">{{cite journal|last1=Wolfaardt|first1=A. C.|last2=Underhill|first2=L. G.|last3=Altwegg|first3=R.|last4=Visagie|first4=J.|year=2008|title=Restoration of oiled African penguins Spheniscus demersus a decade after the Apollo Sea spill|journal=African Journal of Marine Science|volume=30|issue=2|pages=421–436|doi=10.2989/ajms.2008.30.2.14.564|bibcode=2008AfJMS..30..421W |s2cid=85384818}}</ref>

==== 2000 MV ''Treasure'' crisis ====
Disaster struck on 23 June 2000, when the iron ore tanker [[MV Treasure oil spill|MV ''Treasure'' sank]] between [[Robben Island]] and [[Dassen Island]], South Africa. It released {{Convert|400–1,000|t|LT ST|abbr=}} of fuel oil, causing an unprecedented coastal bird crisis and oiling 19,000 adult penguins at the height of the best breeding season on record for this vulnerable species.<ref name="sanccob">{{cite web|url=http://www.sanccob.co.za/?m=2&s=5&idkey=625|title=Penguins, rescue, rehabilitation, research, internships|publisher=Sanccob|access-date=2015-09-25}}</ref> The oiled birds were brought to an abandoned train repair warehouse in Cape Town to be cared for. An additional 19,500 un-oiled penguins were removed from Dassen Island and other areas before they became oiled and were released about 800 kilometres east of Cape Town. This gave workers enough time to clean up the oiled waters and shores before the birds could complete their long swim home (which took the penguins between one and three weeks). Some of the penguins were named and [[GPS wildlife tracking|radio-tracked]] as they swam back to their breeding grounds. Tens of thousands of volunteers helped with the rescue and rehabilitation process, which was overseen by the [[International Fund for Animal Welfare]] (IFAW) and the [[South African Foundation for the Conservation of Coastal Birds]] (SANCCOB) and took more than three months to complete. This was the largest animal rescue event in history; more than 91% of the penguins were successfully rehabilitated and released – an amazing feat that could not have been accomplished without such a tremendous international response.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2009/06/090617-SAF-penguinsfree-video-ap.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090619062958/http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2009/06/090617-SAF-penguinsfree-video-ap.html|archive-date=June 19, 2009|title="Jackass Penguins Freed after Rehab", National Geographic's Video News, June 17, 2009|publisher=National Geographic|date=2009-06-17|access-date=2012-03-30}}</ref>

Due to the positive outcome of African penguins being raised in captivity after tragedies such as the ''Treasure'' oil spill, the species is considered a good "candidate for a captive-breeding programme which aims to release offspring into the wild"; however, worry about the spread of new strains of [[avian malaria]] is a major concern in the situation.<ref name="Barham">{{cite journal|author=Barham, P. J.|author2=L. G. Underhill|author3=R. J. M. Crawford|author4=R. Altewegg|author5=T. M. Leshoro|author6=D. A. Bolton|author7=B. M. Dyer|author8=L. Upfold|name-list-style=amp|year=2008|title=The efficacy of hand-rearing penguin chicks: evidence from African penguins (''Spheniscus demersus'') orphaned in the Treasure oil spill|journal=Bird Conservation International|volume=18|issue=2|doi=10.1017/S0959270908000142|doi-access=free}}</ref>

Bringing the birds inland led to the exposure of penguins to parasites and [[disease vector]]s such as mosquitoes carrying avian malaria, which has caused 27% of the rehabilitated penguin deaths annually.<ref name="Grim">{{cite journal|author=Grim, C.|year=2003 |title=''Plasmodium juxtanucleare'' associated with mortality in black-footed penguins (''Spheniscus demersus'') admitted to a rehabilitation center|journal=Journal of Zoo and Wildlife Medicine|volume=34|issue=3|pages=250–5|pmid=14582786|jstor=20460327|doi=10.1638/02-070|s2cid=41534960 }}</ref>

==== 2016 & 2019 Port of Ngqura ====
Small-scale oil spills (of less than {{Convert|400|l|usgal|abbr=}}) have occurred at the [[Port of Ngqura]] since [[bunkering]] activities started there in 2016. Bunkering is a ship refuelling process that can result in oil spills and oil slicks entering the water. Hundreds of African penguins have been harmed following these spills<ref name="phys">{{Cite web|url=https://phys.org/news/2019-07-refuelling-scrutiny-safrica-penguins-oil.html|title=Refuelling under scrutiny as S. Africa penguins hit by oil spill|website=phys.org|language=en|access-date=2020-04-23}}</ref> due to the port's close proximity to penguin [[Rookery|rookeries]] on [[St. Croix Island (Algoa Bay)|St. Croix Island]] and seabird habitat on neighbouring Jahleel and Brenton Islands.

=== Competition with fisheries ===
The population collapse in the 2000s appears to be largely due to the decrease of food availability, principally [[sardines]] and [[anchovy]] stocks.<ref name=prey>{{Cite journal |last1=Crawford |first1=R. J. M. |last2=David |first2=J. H. M. |last3=Shannon |first3=L. J. |last4=Kemper |first4=J. |last5=Klages |first5=N. T. W. |last6=Roux |first6=J-P. |last7=Underhill |first7=L. G. |last8=Ward |first8=V. L. |last9=Williams |first9=A. J. |last10=Wolfaardt |first10=A. C. |date=2001|title=African penguins as predators and prey – coping (or not) with change |url=https://doi.org/10.2989/025776101784528836 |journal=South African Journal of Marine Science |volume=23 |issue=1 |pages=435–447 |doi=10.2989/025776101784528836 |bibcode=2001SAJMS..23..435C |issn=0257-7615}}</ref> [[Commercial fisheries]] of these fish have forced these penguins to search for prey farther offshore, as well as having to switch to eating less nutritious prey.<ref name=seaturtles/> Restricting commercial fishing near colony sites such as [[Robben Island]] for short periods (3 years) was shown to markedly improve penguin breeding success. Longer closure periods and closures near other colonies are being evaluated.<ref name=sherley2013>{{cite journal|last1=Sherley|first1=R. B.|last2=Underhill|first2=L. G.|last3=Barham|first3=B. J.|last4=Barham|first4=P. J.|last5=Coetzee|first5=J. C.|last6=Crawford|first6=R. J.|last7=Dyer|first7=B. M.|last8=Leshoro|first8=T. M.|last9=Upfold|first9=L.|year=2013|title=Influence of local and regional prey availability on breeding performance of African penguins Spheniscus demersus|journal=Marine Ecology Progress Series|volume=473|pages=291–301|doi=10.3354/meps10070|bibcode=2013MEPS..473..291S|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="weller2016">{{cite journal|title=System dynamics modelling of the Endangered African penguin populations on Dyer and Robben islands, South Africa|first1=Florian|last1=Weller|first2=Richard B.|last2=Sherley|first3=Lauren J.|last3=Waller|first4=Katrin|last4=Ludynia|first5=Deon|last5=Geldenhuys|first6=Lynne J.|last6=Shannon|first7=Astrid|last7=Jarre|journal=Ecological Modelling|volume=327|year=2016|pages=44–56|doi=10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2016.01.011|bibcode=2016EcMod.327...44W }}</ref><ref name=sherley2018>{{cite journal|title=Bayesian inference reveals positive but subtle effects of experimental fishery closures on marine predator demographics|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B|volume=285|issue=1871|article-number=20172443|year=2018|last=Sherley|first=Richard B.|display-authors=etal|doi=10.1098/rspb.2017.2443|pmid=29343602|pmc=5805942 |bibcode=2018PBioS.28572443S }}</ref>

Fishing pressure and climate variability have engendered major ecological changes in the Benguela system. African penguins are highly sensitive to any type of decline in sardine and anchovies. Current food availability is considered to now limit colonies to relatively small sizes.<ref name=prey/> 

=== Cape fur seal predation ===
Sustained conservation efforts to increase the number of [[Cape fur seals]] are thought to have contributed to the decline of African penguin populations. In Namibia, breeding colonies have been decimated by the predation of "rogue" Cape fur seals on African penguins. Penguins on [[Dyer Island (South Africa)|Dyer Island]] in South Africa face the same threat. Conservationists consider culling of seals near breeding colonies as an effective recourse.<ref name="weller2016"/> Observational results from [[Mercury Island]] indicate that seal culling led to an increase in the population of penguins.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ferreira |first=Anton |date=2024-07-24 |title=Seal attacks contribute to African penguin's looming extinction |url=https://groundup.org.za/article/seal-attacks-contribute-to-demise-african-penguin/ |access-date=2025-03-19 |website=[[GroundUp (news agency)|GroundUp News]] |language=en-ZA}}</ref>

===Conservation status===
The African penguin is one of the species to which the [[African-Eurasian Waterbird Agreement]] (AEWA) applies. In September 2010, it was listed as endangered under the US [[Endangered Species Act]].<ref name="seaturtles" /> As of 2024, the African penguin is listed as [[critically endangered]] on the [[IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|IUCN Red List]], with the remaining mature individuals around 19,800 birds in a declining population.<ref name="IUCN 2024"/>

===Mediation efforts===
Many organisations such as [[SANCCOB]], Dyer Island Conservation Trust, [[South African Mineral Reporting Codes#SAMREC|SAMREC]], The [[National Aviary]] in Pittsburgh, and Raggy Charters with the Penguin Research Fund in Port Elizabeth are working to halt the decline of the African penguin. Measures include: monitoring population trends, hand-rearing and releasing abandoned chicks, establishing artificial nests and proclaiming marine reserves in which fishing is prohibited.<ref name="IUCN 2024"/> Some colonies (such as on [[Gansbaai#Dyer Island, Geyser Rock and Shark Alley|Dyer Island]]) are suspected to be under heavy pressure from predation by [[Cape fur seals]] and may benefit from the culling of individual problem animals,<ref name="weller2016"/><ref name=Ludynia>{{cite journal|author=Ludynia, K.|author2=L. J. Waller|author3=R. B. Sherley|author4=F. Abadi|author5=Y. Galada|author6=D. Geldenhuys|author7=R. J. M. Crawford|author8=L. J. Shannon|author9=A. Jarre|name-list-style=amp|year=2014|title=Processes influencing the population dynamics and conservation of African penguins on Dyer Island, South Africa|journal=African Journal of Marine Science|volume=36|issue=2|page=253|doi=10.2989/1814232X.2014.929027|bibcode=2014AfJMS..36..253L |s2cid=84659321}}</ref> which has been found effective (although requiring a large amount of management effort) in trials.<ref name=Makhado>{{cite journal|author=Makhado, A. B.|author2=M. A. Meÿer|author3=R. J. M. Crawford|author4=L. G. Underhill|author5=C. Wilke|name-list-style=amp|year=2009|title=The efficacy of culling seals seen preying on seabirds as a means of reducing seabird mortality|journal=African Journal of Ecology|volume=47|issue=3|page=335|doi=10.1111/j.1365-2028.2008.00966.x|bibcode=2009AfJEc..47..335M |hdl=11427/24160|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

Established in 1968, SANCCOB is currently the only organisation mandated by the South African government to respond to crises involving seabirds along South Africa's coastline and is internationally recognised for the role it played during the MV ''Treasure'' oil spill. A modelling exercise conducted in 2003 by the [[University of Cape Town]]'s FitzPatrick Institute of African Ornithology found that rehabilitating oiled African penguins has resulted in the current population being 19% larger than it would have been in the absence of SANCCOB's rehabilitation efforts.<ref name="Rehab Penguins">{{cite book|author1=Nel, D. C.|author2=Crawford, R. J. M.|author3=Parsons, N. J.|year=2003|chapter=The conservation status and impact of oiling on the African penguin|title=Rehabilitation of Oiled African Penguins: a Conservation Success Story|editor=Nel, D. C.|editor2=Whittington, P. A.|location=Cape Town|publisher=BirdLife South Africa and Avian Demography Unit|pages=1–7}}</ref>

In February 2015, the Dyer Island Conservation Trust opened the African Penguin and Seabird Sanctuary (APSS) in [[Gansbaai]], South Africa.<ref name=ScenicSouth>{{cite web|url=http://scenicsouth.co.za/first-class-african-penguin-and-seabird-sanctuary-opens-in-kleinbaai-overberg/|title=First Class African Penguin and Seabird Sanctuary Opens in Kleinbaai, Overberg|publisher=SceinicSouth|date=2015-02-26|access-date=2015-03-12}}</ref> The centre was opened by then-[[Department of Tourism (South Africa)|Department of Tourism]] minister [[Derek Hanekom]]<ref name=iol>{{cite web|url=http://www.iol.co.za/scitech/science/environment/rehab-centre-goes-to-the-birds-1.1826123#.VQGFMfzF-So|title="Rehab center goes to the birds", iol SciTech, March 3, 2015|publisher=iol.co.za |date=2015-03-03|access-date=2015-03-12}}</ref> and will serve as a hub for seabird research carried out by the Dyer Island Conservation Trust. The centre will also run local education projects, host international marine volunteers and seek to improve seabird handling techniques and rehabilitation protocols.

==Captivity==
[[File:Dallas World Aquarium January 2019 14 (black-footed penguin).jpg|thumb|African penguin at the [[Dallas World Aquarium]], [[Dallas]], [[Texas]], United States]]

African penguins are a commonly seen species in zoos across the world. Because they do not require particularly low temperatures, they are often kept in outside enclosures. They adapt fairly well to this captive environment and are rather easy to breed compared to other species of the family. In [[Europe]], the breeding programme [[EAZA Ex-situ Programme|EAZA]] is regulated by [[Natura Artis Magistra|Artis Royal Zoo]] in the [[Netherlands]], whilst in the United States the [[Species Survival Plan|SSP]] program is cooperatively managed by the [[Association of Zoos and Aquariums|AZA]]. The idea is to create a backup captive population, as well as to aid in the conservation of the population in its natural habitat. Between 2010 and 2013, American zoos spent $300,000 on ''[[in situ]]'' (wild population) conservation.<ref name=aza>{{Cite web|url=https://www.aza.org/SAFE-african-penguin|title=African Penguin|publisher=aza.org}}</ref><ref name=artis>{{Cite web|url=https://www.artis.nl/en/footer/about-artis/nature-conservation/nature-conservation-project-african-penguin/|title=Nature conservation project African penguin|publisher=artis.nl}}</ref>

斑嘴環企鵝（學名：Spheniscus demersus），又名南非企鵝、非洲企鵝、公驢企鵝或黑腳企鵝，是一種生活在南部非洲沿岸的環企鵝屬鳥類。牠們僅分布於南部非洲，也是唯一一種分布於非洲的企鵝。牠們是魚食性，主要以沙丁魚或鯷魚為食，並生活在南非跟納米比亞沿岸的海岸或離島上。叫聲類似於驢子因而得名「公驢企鵝」此一暱稱。
斑嘴環企鵝的體長約60—70公分，體重約2.1—3.7公斤，壽命約10—27年，體色主要是黑白雙色並輔以部分粉紅色，特徵是其胸上一條馬蹄形的黑色寬帶，以及黑白相間的臉部圖案。雄雌差異不大，求偶時有特殊的儀式，行一夫一妻制。雌鳥一次產2顆卵，並由雙親輪流照顧蛋，在38—40天後孵化。在60—130天內開始更換為亞成鳥的羽毛，並在10—22個月後換為成鳥羽毛，並在約四歲時性成熟並繁殖。
牠們除了非洲毛皮海獅及黑背鷗等天敵之外，也備受環境變遷、人為干擾及污染帶來的危害。油污及噪音的汙染導致牠們不能正常覓食及溝通，環境的變遷使魚群改變位置而不易覓食，食物的減少亦導致其繁殖率的下降。其數量自1930年代的57萬對企鵝下滑至2019年的2.1萬對，並且沒有停止的趨勢，因此被《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》列入瀕危物種。


物種命名
斑嘴環企鵝最早在1747年可由英國的自然學家喬治·愛德華的《罕見鳥類的自然史》（A Natural History of Uncommon Birds）第二卷中找到紀錄，他仔細地描述其特徵並稱之「黑腳企鵝」（Black-Footed Penguins）。愛德華在文章提到他並無法確認其標本採集地點，但從捐贈者得知是從東印度船隻運送的，進而猜測這些標本來自好望角地區。卡爾·林奈在1758年引用了愛德華的資料並在《自然系統》第十版中以二名法命名為Diomedea demersa，當時與漂泊信天翁一同歸類在大信天翁屬下。後來於1760年，法國動物學家馬蒂蘭·雅克·布里松建立了環企鵝屬並將其作為該屬的模式種。
Spheniscus來自於希臘文sphen或sphenos，意為楔形；這指的是這物種的楔形翅膀，翅膀的構形提供了強大的推進力量，讓斑嘴環企鵝在游泳時能迅速前進。demersus是拉丁文潛入水中的意思，demersa為其變體。

由古化石的研究中，斑嘴環企鵝並非由過去在當地的祖先演化而成，而是比較有可能從南美洲由洋流將其帶至非洲演化的，解釋了為何非洲現存企鵝只有斑嘴環企鵝的可能原因。目前沒有亞種分化，是單型種。


形態描述
斑嘴環企鵝體長60—70公分，體重2.1—3.7公斤之間（平均3.1公斤），跗骨平均長32.3公分，翼展平均98.5公分。野生個體壽命約10—27年，圈養環境下可更長。

斑嘴環企鵝成鳥主要為黑白兩色，並有少許粉紅色。虹膜為深褐色，眼圈為粉紅色，面部黑色，喙黑、有時在尖端帶有深灰色、並在接近尖端的地方帶有一條垂直的淡灰色條紋（少數狀況下為白色），臉部至脖子間有一條寬白色帶。在極熱的環境條件下，眼睛上方的淡白色裸露皮膚會變成鮮亮的粉紅色環繞眼睛。翅膀外側及背部羽毛呈黑色，並延伸至腋窩下，翅膀內側顏色較為多變，可能有黑、白、粉色三色交雜的斑點。胸部和腹部則有一些帶黑色斑紋的白色羽毛，並在胸上有一條馬蹄形的黑色帶子。腿偏黑色，可能帶有一些淡粉色斑點，黑色尾部上有一個白色斑點。這種黑白羽毛構成了一種對捕食者的保護色，從水下看，白色羽毛對水生捕食者具有迷惑作用，而從空中看，黑色羽毛對空中捕食者具有迷惑作用。

兩性之間差異不大，需要在一對鳥一起時才容易分辨性別。但一般而言雄性體型較大，並可依照嘴的長度和深度成功推測其90%以上的性別。

亞成鳥喙呈黑灰色，淡色帶可能不明顯。眼睛或喙上方有較為清晰的白色斑點或短線，側面的顏色較淡，喉嚨由深色過渡至更接近白色。前頸呈白色，可能帶有部分灰黑色或者整體顏色偏暗，腹部為白色夾雜一些黑色斑點。幼鳥上半身呈深藍灰或深灰色，後來會轉為褐色。胸部條紋不完整。腿呈淡粉灰色至暗灰色至黑色皆有。在第二和第三年時會顯示出不同程度的成年面部圖案。
斑嘴環企鵝與麥哲倫環企鵝及洪保德環企鵝關係相近且長相相似，牠們在圈養環境下可以雜交。有些斑嘴環企鵝個體甚至會表現出類似於麥哲倫環企鵝胸部的雙層黑色條紋特徵，但該企鵝從未在非洲有被確認的紀錄。但麥哲倫環企鵝少有脖子上的粗白條紋且頭較圓，在接近喙的基部也有較多羽毛；而洪保德環企鵝的粉色面積較明顯，但不延伸至下頷骨，頭部的構型也有差異。


棲息地與分佈

斑嘴環企鵝主要在納米比亞及南非的海岸地區，西到安哥拉南部，東至夸祖魯-納塔爾省。並可作為零星的過境鳥出現在西非加彭的Setté Cama至東非莫三比克的林波波河口。此區域位於本格拉寒流及阿古拉斯洋流形成的生態系統中，並主要由本格拉寒流提供營養。
在2009年的統計中，斑嘴環企鵝一共有29個繁殖地點，包含了25個島嶼和4個非洲大陸海岸，其中有80%的企鵝分佈於其中7個島嶼上。在南非最主要的地點是達森島；在納米比亞則為墨丘利島。


習性
斑嘴環企鵝為日行性，大部分的時間用在覓食上，晚上會在岸上群聚過夜。會互相理羽，或者至離岸數公尺處洗澡以保持清潔及去除寄生蟲。牠們平均潛水深度為30公尺，最深可達130公尺，潛水時間可達4分35秒，泳速可達每小時20公里。雄雌之間最大潛水能力相似，但雄性平均潛水深度和時間比雌性更深、更長；雌性以增加潛水頻率補救該落差，就結果而言兩者探索水體體積相似，而雌性搜尋的範圍可更廣。成鳥通常不遷徙，就算會因為覓食而移動也不會不遠，並在繁殖時返回原處；但亞成鳥會有沿著海岸線向西和向北散佈的行為。
繁殖期時，在約離繁殖地30—40公里範圍內覓食，最遠至離岸70公里處。非繁殖期時，亞成鳥可以藉此旅行600—700公里遠，但也有標記個體的研究發現有數隻成年個體從900—1000公里外的納米比亞墨丘利島移動到南非羅本島繁殖，但放歸時其出生地未知。


食性
牠們為魚食性，高度仰賴以50—120公釐長的沙丁魚和鯷魚如南非鯷等遠洋魚群為食，極限範圍為10—310公釐。牠們也以南非竹筴魚、沙丁脂眼鯡、雙鬚多棘鰕虎、懷氏脂眼鯡及其他烏賊等頭足類和甲殼類為食。而幼鳥則以魚苗為食。
可觀察到25—165隻成鳥會在海上成群活動，一起合作潛水將魚群圍捕起來覓食的行為。根據一份對1980年代的研究，在不含南非東側的情況下，斑嘴環企鵝每年估計約消耗3171公噸的食物。


繁殖
斑嘴環企鵝為一夫一妻制，並如同其他企鵝以群體繁殖為主。雖然斑嘴環企鵝全年都能繁殖，但仍有數個月份是其高峰：納米比亞的高峰期為十一月至十二月，而南非為三月至五月，牠們繁殖時會返回之前的繁殖地繁殖。
求偶儀式分為數個階段，通常以雄性進行視覺和聽覺表演來吸引配偶開始。頭擺動通常表示對巢穴位置的擁有權，吸引雌性，或者作為對其他雄性的警告。第二階段用於確保建立共同的聯繫，這包括在頸部和頭部向上伸展時發出的尖銳聲音。在最後一個階段時，其中一隻或兩隻企鵝低頭鞠躬，喙指向巢穴或另一隻鳥的腳。

成功配對後，牠們會在鳥糞、岩石、沙地或稀疏的植被下找尋合適的地方挖掘淺淺的巢穴，並有可能在內陸移動超過一公里以尋找地點。也可以利用埋入地面的管道和箱子製成的人工巢穴做巢。一次產兩顆卵，每顆間隔三天。蛋由雙親輪流利用腹部的孵卵斑孵蛋，平均約兩天半輪替一次，約38—40天後孵化。

蛋孵化後的頭15天內，雙親會繼續輪班覆蓋著幼鳥以保持其體溫。牠們會通過反芻食物到幼鳥嘴裡，為其餵食約一個月的時間。到了這時雙親會離開幼鳥去覓食，而幼鳥們會聚集成群避免被天敵或者有敵意的其他成鳥攻擊。幼鳥在60—130天內開始換成亞成鳥的羽毛，隨後離開繁殖地。
亞成鳥會在外遊蕩約12到22個月，並事先囤積約五週的脂肪再返回出生地，準備更換為成鳥羽毛。此時的時間點依照不同區域會有所不同：南非的企鵝會在11月至1月之間換羽；在納米比亞則在4月和5月之間。亞成鳥會在岸上停留約20—35天等待換羽完畢再開始覓食，這時體重可能會下降了一半，並需要約要六週的時間回復。這些成鳥在2至6歲之間會進行初次繁殖，通常在4歲時達到高峰。
其繁殖後代的性別比例與當年食物供應多寡有關，若當年食物對繁殖有利，則後代雄性比例較高，這可能是因為雄性個體有更快的生長速度和離巢體重，較受益於食物豐富的影響，而雌性需要花費更多時間覓食但探索範圍較廣，造成投資成本的問題。


叫聲
班嘴環企鵝可聽見100到15000赫茲之間的聲音。牠們的語言模式同人類大多數語言遵照簡潔定律以及曼澤拉斯定律，即越常用到的字或者句子跟鳴叫聲愈長，音節就會越短。求偶的聲音類似驢子叫，因而得到「公驢企鵝」（jackass penguin）此一暱稱。在夜間時可聽到其不同叫聲。牠們也會利用叫聲溝通增加在海中行群體捕食的效率。


天敵與威脅
班嘴環企鵝主要的天敵有非洲毛皮海獅、黑背鷗、埃及聖䴉、筆尾獴、大斑獛和豹等，若企鵝受到干擾並被迫離開巢穴，蛋跟幼鳥被黑背鷗掠食的情形會更進一步惡化。並發現數種寄生蟲會寄生於血液內，如殘瘧原蟲。在非洲陸地上的繁殖點也容易遭到其他鳥類傳染疾病，博爾德斯海灘的斑嘴環企鵝群體在2022年爆發禽流感並造成至少28隻企鵝死亡。也曾有遭海角蜜蜂攻擊導致63隻企鵝死亡的報導。
而面臨的威脅有海洋環境汙染、食物減少、與其他動物搶食的惡劣環境、人類活動、外來掠食者以及氣候變遷等。有研究認為因為漁業跟氣候變遷，這些企鵝可能已經陷入生態陷阱內。


食物來源缺乏
食物來源品質降低及喜歡吃的食物減少是斑嘴環企鵝繁殖成功率低的主要因素之一。受到聖嬰現象、阿古拉斯洋流的移動以及風向的變化，本格拉寒流主要的沙丁魚群及鯷魚群由南海岸移動到了納米比亞的呂德里茨外海或東部外海區域，對這些企鵝過於遙遠。在1990年代末—2000年代初之間，這兩種魚群的數量增加，但在2004年之後，沙丁魚的生物量降至低於平均水平。食物的供應多寡決定了育雛成功率和雛鳥的離巢率，因此導致企鵝的數量下降。
沙丁魚及鯷魚也是附近圍網漁業的捕撈目標。小型遠洋漁船圍繞沙丁魚繁殖區域，造成沙丁魚大量減產，並導致企鵝的數量也跟著減少。


油汙染
歷年來在當地大小油外洩事故的發生，往往使企鵝的羽毛結塊而導致隔熱性及隔水性下降，且無法待在低溫的海洋裡。這些鳥類會因為無法進食而脫水，一周內可能失去多達13％的體重，因此最終餓死。企鵝嘗試除去這些油污的過程中也可能因此吞下這些有害的油污而導致失明和潰瘍。
1968年時，在埃索·埃森號（Esso Essen）撞擊水下物體擱淺後，使約4000公噸的石油洩露至海洋中，導致3000隻企鵝以及其他海鳥遭到汙染。1971年在干斯拜的油輪漏油事故導致10000噸原油向海洋洩漏並導致超過1200隻企鵝被汙染。在1972年，一次來源不明的泄漏事件導致4000隻企鵝被油污。在1970年至1980年期間，已知至少有7088隻企鵝受到原油和部分來自魚油的影響。1994年，阿波羅海號散貨船在達森島附近沉沒，使約10000隻企鵝被浮油污染。1985年，鄰近阿爾哥亞灣的勒西菲角也發生過一艘巴拿馬籍散貨船燃油意外洩漏的事故，並使1043隻被汙染及137隻企鵝死亡。
2000年時，在羅本島與達森島之間發生一起嚴重的漏油事故，造成約19000隻企鵝遭到油汙染，其中有14825隻來自羅本島，3516隻來自達森島。該船承載了約1400公噸的燃油，其中1344公噸是重油，還有56公噸的船用柴油以及64公噸的潤滑油，被稱為是南非最嚴重的環境災難。該次事故造成所有被油污染的企鵝送到南部非洲沿海鳥類保育基金會清洗並治療，還有19506隻未被污染的企鵝從原先在南非西南側的棲息地被暫時移動到東南側的吉科巴哈附近以清除海灘上的油污。


海鳥糞或鳥蛋的人為干擾
海鳥糞富含氮和磷酸鹽，是自印加人發現其開採價值以來世界上第一個商業化肥料。班嘴環企鵝也偏好在這種鳥糞或沙土中的挖洞築巢，但在缺乏這些合適地點時，牠們會在灌木叢裡、巨石下、建築物上或裸露的地面上築巢。這些在地面所築的鳥巢容易暴露於極端炎熱的沙漠風暴中，且容易受到海鷗攻擊，影響其繁殖成功率。而開採海鳥糞後造成的凹地也容易造成積水並導致巢穴被淹。
人為干擾和採集蛋也是數量下降的因素：遊客可能會導致巢穴坍塌，大量的遊客可能會阻礙幼鳥的繁殖。也有數以千計的企鵝蛋被撿走並在附近的城鎮出售。在1953年內，就有12000顆蛋從南非被出口銷售。而在1965年的報章中提及有3000—4000打的企鵝蛋被出售至美食家的餐桌上。而直到1968年，南非國會還在供應企鵝蛋作為早餐選項。
在船長班傑明·莫雷爾於1828年的航行中發現伊查博島上的鳥糞礦後，有商人開始採集南非沿岸島嶼的鳥糞，並同時讓水手食用那些企鵝蛋。到了1845年，一共有30萬公噸的鳥糞被銷售至英國，並留下空空如也的島嶼及被風羽跟鷗鳥摧殘的企鵝。


其他問題
噪音汙染的增加會影響包含企鵝在內的海洋動物們尋找和捕捉獵物、溝通、定向、躲避或育幼的能力，且班嘴環企鵝對噪音干擾非常敏感。南非外海的聖克羅伊島曾經是斑嘴環企鵝們最大的繁殖地之一，但自從2016年起當地政府允許船隻在該海域海上加油以來，噪音水平增加了一倍、同時也增加了船隻漏油的風險，而當地的企鵝數量則從2016年的8500對下降至2022年的1200對。


數量與保護狀況
班嘴環企鵝曾經是南非數量最多的海鳥，但數量受到各種威脅而不斷下降。1930年的調查有57萬對企鵝，1956年時有14萬對，到了2000年整體約為6.3萬對，而2019年的調查僅餘20850對。納米比亞的數量由1978年的12162對下降到2015年約5800對；南非的非洲企鵝數量從1978年的約70000對下降到2015年的19300對，到了2024年僅剩不到10000對。其數量下降到僅剩原先族群10%的趨勢因為仍沒有停止的跡象，因此被列入瀕危物種。
當地環境部門保護企鵝棲息地不受干擾、確保牠們食物豐富、在靠近魚場附近建立新的養殖場，並建造了人工巢穴以增加其繁殖地。在南非，每年都會對所有企鵝繁殖地監測其數量趨勢，其大多繁殖區域為國家公園或者自然保護區，並限制在該地採集鳥糞石或蛋，且制定《非洲企鵝生物多樣性管理計劃》（African Penguin Biodiversity Management Plan）指導保育行動。2024年時南非政府在6個南非企鵝棲息地實施禁漁政策以保障企鵝食物來源。在達森島上，採蛋在1967年後被禁止，在1969年完全禁止收集任何蛋用以販售。在納米比亞，其監測頻率較低，但在2009年成立了海洋保護區以保護其超過10000平方公里的繁殖地和重要的覓食地。
南部非洲沿海鳥類保育基金會致力於保護和重新引入鳥類，他們將受油污的企鵝清潔並治療後放回環境，目前有超過80％的企鵝成功返回野外生活。人工飼養被放棄或存活機會不大的巢穴中的幼鳥也使數量增加了7000隻。

與人類的關係
非洲企鵝具有經濟方面的價值，牠們能夠吸引遊客前來觀賞，世界各地皆有動物園有飼養斑嘴環企鵝。在1995年9月至1996年2月期間，有16萬人次參觀了博爾德斯海岸公園（Boulders Coastal Park），其中大多數的遊客是為了觀賞企鵝前來。而在2015年的報導中指出每年平均有近60萬來自全球的遊客會至該地參訪。
南部非洲沿海鳥類保育基金會也規劃讓學生有機會學習自然和與污染相關的威脅。
